questions.q: An assistant must answer from a set of documents the base LLM was never trained on. What does RAG do?
options: Retrieve the matching passages, then generate from them
Answer straight from the model's memory and hope it fits
Retrain the whole model on the documents every night
Look the question up in a plain keyword index only
questions.explain: RAG retrieves relevant text, then the LLM generates an answer grounded in that text.
questions.hint: RAG has two steps — one FINDS text, the other WRITES the answer.
questions.explain_simple: The computer first SEARCHES your documents for the parts that match the question. Then it gives those parts to the AI, and the AI WRITES the answer using them. Search first, then write.

questions.q: When are answers best handled by RAG rather than a plain LLM?
options: When the documents are short enough to memorize
When the answers live in your own private documents
When you only need the model to reply more quickly
When you want to drop the language model entirely
questions.explain: RAG fits when the knowledge is in your own documents, not in the model's training data.
questions.hint: Think about WHERE the answer lives — inside the model, or in your files?
questions.explain_simple: Use RAG when the answer is inside YOUR documents that the AI never studied. RAG lets the AI read your files at the moment of the question, so it can answer about things it was never trained on.

questions.q: Put the RAG build steps in the order the notebook uses them.
options: Generate, retrieve, embed, chunk, then load files
Embed, generate, load, chunk, then store vectors
Load, chunk, embed, store, retrieve, then generate
Chunk, generate, load, retrieve, then embed text
questions.explain: The order is: load docs, chunk, embed, store in the vector DB, then retrieve and generate.
questions.hint: You must prepare and store the text BEFORE you can search it.
questions.explain_simple: First load the documents, cut them into small pieces (chunk), turn each piece into numbers (embed), and save them. Only THEN can you search (retrieve) and let the AI write (generate). Prepare → store → search → answer.

questions.q: Long documents are split into 'chunks' before indexing. Why?
options: Smaller pieces are simply easier for a person to read
The database rejects any file above one kilobyte
Chunking translates the text into English for you
Small pieces retrieve precisely and fit the context
questions.explain: Chunking returns only relevant passages and keeps pieces within the context window.
questions.hint: Do you want the whole book, or just the one page you need?
questions.explain_simple: Documents are long. We cut them into small pieces so the search returns just the piece that answers the question, not a huge document. Small pieces = precise answers and they fit the AI's memory.

questions.q: If CHUNK_SIZE is very large (a whole document per chunk), what is the main downside?
options: Retrieved text carries noise and costs more tokens
The embeddings suddenly become perfectly accurate
The vector database refuses to store the big chunks
The final answer becomes guaranteed to be correct
questions.explain: Chunks that are too large bring unrelated text, adding noise and token cost.
questions.hint: A big piece brings extra text you never asked for.
questions.explain_simple: If each piece is too big, the search brings back lots of extra text that is not about the question. That extra text confuses the AI and costs more (more tokens). Smaller, focused pieces work better.

questions.q: What is the purpose of CHUNK_OVERLAP between consecutive chunks?
options: It shrinks each chunk further to save on memory
It keeps ideas on a boundary from being cut in half
It randomly shuffles the words inside each chunk
It scans for and deletes duplicate source files
questions.explain: Overlap repeats a little text at boundaries so an idea split across chunks stays findable.
questions.hint: What happens if an idea is cut right where you split the text?
questions.explain_simple: When we cut text into pieces, an idea can be cut in the middle. Overlap means each piece repeats a little of the piece before it, so an idea that sits on the cut line is still complete in at least one piece.

questions.q: Each chunk is turned into an embedding. What is an embedding?
options: A zipped, compressed copy of the whole document
The plain English translation of the text chunk
A list of numbers standing for the text's meaning
A random identifier handed to each stored chunk
questions.explain: An embedding is a numeric vector capturing meaning; similar meaning gives similar vectors.
questions.hint: The computer needs NUMBERS, not words, to compare meaning.
questions.explain_simple: An embedding is text turned into a list of numbers that represents its MEANING. Two texts with similar meaning get similar numbers. This lets the computer compare meanings by comparing numbers.

questions.q: Why embed the text instead of matching keywords?
options: It works only for images and never for plain text
Keyword matching is impossible to write in Python
It makes every stored document noticeably shorter
It matches by meaning even when the words differ
questions.explain: Embeddings enable semantic search — matching by meaning, not exact keywords.
questions.hint: Same meaning but different words — can exact-word search catch that?
questions.explain_simple: Keyword search only finds exact words. Embeddings find MEANING, so a question can match the right text even when it uses different words (like 'car' and 'automobile'). Meaning beats exact words.

questions.q: The lab sets EMBEDDING_MODEL = "text-embedding-3-small". What is its job?
options: Turn text into meaning-vectors for the similarity search
Write the final natural-language answer for the users
Store the finished vectors safely onto the local disk
Split the long documents into the smaller text chunks
questions.explain: text-embedding-3-small converts text into vectors; generation is a separate model.
questions.hint: This model MAKES the number-vectors — it does not write answers.
questions.explain_simple: text-embedding-3-small has one job: turn text into number-vectors so we can search by meaning. It does NOT write the answer — a different model (GPT) does that part.

questions.q: Why embed the question with the SAME model used for the chunks?
options: It is only a matter of personal coding-style choice
Similarity holds only if both share one vector space
Other models are faster but return identical vectors
The question does not need any embedding in the first
questions.explain: Query and documents must use the same embedding model so their vectors are comparable.
questions.hint: You can only compare numbers that were made the SAME way.
questions.explain_simple: The documents and the question must be turned into numbers by the SAME model. If you use two different models, the numbers do not line up, and comparing them for similarity becomes meaningless.

questions.q: The lab uses ChromaDB with get_or_create_collection(...). What does this vector database do?
options: Run the language model that writes out the answers
Render the Gradio interface shown to the end user
Store chunk embeddings and find ones close to a query
Download the source documents from across the web
questions.explain: ChromaDB stores embeddings and performs nearest-neighbor similarity search.
questions.hint: It is a special storage that searches by CLOSENESS.
questions.explain_simple: The vector database (ChromaDB) stores all the number-vectors and quickly finds the ones most similar to your question. It is the 'search' part — it does not write answers or draw the screen.

questions.q: Why store chunks in a vector database instead of a normal SQL text table?
options: A normal SQL table cannot store any kind of text
Vector stores are always cheaper to run than SQL
SQL databases delete their own stored rows over time
It is built for similarity search across embeddings
questions.explain: Vector DBs are optimized for nearest-neighbor search over embeddings.
questions.hint: A normal database matches exact values; this one matches closeness.
questions.explain_simple: A normal SQL database looks for EXACT matches. A vector database is built to find the CLOSEST meanings (nearest numbers). Semantic search needs closeness, so we use a vector database.

questions.q: collection.add(embeddings=..., documents=..., ids=...) runs after embedding. What just happened?
options: Each chunk, its vector, and an id became searchable
The final answer was delivered to the waiting user
The source documents were removed from the store
The model was fine-tuned on the newly added chunks
questions.explain: collection.add indexes chunks with embeddings and ids so they can be retrieved.
questions.hint: This line SAVES things so they can be found later.
questions.explain_simple: collection.add saves each chunk together with its numbers (embedding) and an id. After this line runs, those chunks are stored and ready to be searched when a question comes in.

questions.q: The code embeds the question, then calls collection.query(query_embeddings=[...]). What is this?
options: Writing the full answer with the GPT generation model
Embedding the question and finding the closest chunks
Retraining the embedding model on the user's question
Deleting the whole collection before it answers back
questions.explain: Retrieval embeds the question, then queries the vector DB for the closest chunks.
questions.hint: First turn the QUESTION into numbers, then look for near ones.
questions.explain_simple: When a question arrives, we turn it into numbers too, then ask the database: 'which stored chunks have the closest numbers?' Those closest chunks are the ones most likely to hold the answer.

questions.q: The lab sets TOP_K = 8 and passes n_results=top_k. What does TOP_K control?
options: How many words the final answer is allowed to hold
How many times the model is fine-tuned per question
How many close chunks are pulled in as the context
The temperature setting used by the generation model
questions.explain: TOP_K / n_results is the number of nearest chunks passed to the generator.
questions.hint: K is just a COUNT of how many pieces you grab.
questions.explain_simple: TOP_K is simply HOW MANY of the closest chunks you take. TOP_K = 8 means take the 8 most similar pieces and give them to the AI as background to answer from.

questions.q: A student sets TOP_K = 1. What risk does this create?
options: It guarantees the fastest and the fullest answers
It makes the resulting embeddings far more precise
It forces the model to hallucinate the whole answer
A single chunk may miss context the answer needs
questions.explain: Too small a TOP_K can miss relevant context; too large adds noise. It's a balance.
questions.hint: Only one piece — what if the answer needs two?
questions.explain_simple: If you take only 1 chunk and the answer actually needs two, the AI will not have enough. Too few pieces can miss the answer; too many add noise. You need a balance.

questions.q: After retrieval, chunks go into the prompt and gpt-4o-mini is called. What is the generation step?
options: The model writes an answer from the retrieved chunks
The model stores new embeddings back into ChromaDB
The model splits the documents into smaller chunks
The model computes all of the cosine-similarity scores
questions.explain: Generation is where the LLM composes the final answer from the retrieved context.
questions.hint: This is the step that actually WRITES the sentence.
questions.explain_simple: Generation is when the AI (GPT) takes the pieces we found and WRITES the final answer in normal language, using those pieces as its source.

questions.q: Why tell the model to answer using the retrieved context, not its memory?
options: To make the final answer noticeably longer for users
To ground the answer in sources and cut hallucination
Because the generation model holds no training data
To avoid having to use any embeddings in the pipeline
questions.explain: Grounding the answer in retrieved text keeps it faithful and reduces made-up facts.
questions.hint: Should the AI guess, or use the documents we handed it?
questions.explain_simple: We tell the AI to answer ONLY from the pieces we found, not from its own memory. This keeps the answer true to your documents and stops the AI from making things up (hallucinating).

questions.q: The documents are updated with new information. With RAG, what is the easiest correct fix?
options: Fine-tune the whole model again from scratch each night
Rewrite the entire application in a different language
Re-chunk and re-embed the new documents into the store
Do nothing, because the model already knows the update
questions.explain: With RAG you just index new documents; no model retraining is needed.
questions.hint: With RAG, do you retrain the model, or just add the files?
questions.explain_simple: When documents change, RAG is easy: you just cut and save the new documents into the database. You do NOT retrain the AI — that is the big advantage of RAG.

questions.q: When is fine-tuning a better choice than RAG?
options: Whenever the source documents change very frequently
When you want to skip any training cost whatsoever
When answers must be grounded in specific documents
When you must change the model's style or behavior
questions.explain: Fine-tuning changes behavior baked into the weights; RAG supplies fresh grounded facts.
questions.hint: Facts to look up → RAG. Changing HOW it talks → ?
questions.explain_simple: Fine-tuning is better when you want to change HOW the model behaves or its style — not to give it facts to look up. For fresh facts from documents, use RAG.

questions.q: This is the 'AI Prompt Edition' and students used an LLM to write code. Does that make it RAG?
options: No — coding with an LLM is separate from being RAG
Yes — any use at all of an LLM counts fully as RAG
No — it is really a fine-tuning project underneath
Yes — writing code with AI is the meaning of retrieval
questions.explain: Using an LLM to help write code is unrelated; it's RAG because it retrieves then generates.
questions.hint: Using AI to WRITE the code is not the same as what the app DOES.
questions.explain_simple: Students used an AI to help write the code — that is just a coding helper. The PROJECT is RAG because of what it DOES: search documents, then generate an answer. How the code was written does not decide that.

questions.q: A user's question returns an irrelevant answer. Which cause fits the RAG pipeline best?
options: The temporary Gradio share link had simply expired
Retrieval pulled the wrong chunks for that question
The embedding model translated the text by mistake
The vector database always returns totally random chunks
questions.explain: Bad RAG answers usually trace to retrieval — chunking, embeddings, or TOP_K bringing wrong context.
questions.hint: If the answer is wrong, which step probably grabbed the wrong text?
questions.explain_simple: If the answer is off-topic, the SEARCH (retrieval) probably grabbed the wrong pieces — maybe bad chunking, weak embeddings, or the wrong TOP_K. Fix retrieval and the answer usually gets better.
